annotations.label: Uncountable set

anchor.snippet: Such sets are now called uncountable sets

annotations.note: The `Uncountable` typeclass is defined as a type that is not countable, matching the article's notion.

annotations.label: Diagonalization lemma

anchor.snippet: an element s of T can be constructed that doesn't correspond to any

annotations.note: `cantor_surjective` constructs the diagonal set `{a | a ∉ f a}` not in the range of any `f : α → Set α`, exactly the diagonalization construction.

annotations.label: T is uncountable

anchor.snippet: The set T is uncountable

annotations.note: Taking `α = ℕ`, `cantor_surjective` gives no surjection `ℕ → Set ℕ ≃ (ℕ → Bool)`, i.e. the set of binary strings is uncountable.

annotations.label: R is uncountable

anchor.snippet: Therefore, R is uncountable

annotations.note: `not_countable_real` (and the `Uncountable ℝ` instance) state that the reals are not countable.

annotations.label: Injection from T to R

anchor.snippet: is an injection because it maps different strings to different numbers

annotations.note: `cantorFunction_injective` shows `cantorFunction c : (ℕ → Bool) → ℝ` is injective for `0 < c < 1/2`, an explicit injection from binary strings to reals.

annotations.label: Bijection between T and R

anchor.snippet: Constructing a bijection between T and R is slightly more complicated

annotations.note: `mk_real : #ℝ = 𝔠 = 2^ℵ₀ = #(ℕ → Bool)` gives a (non-constructive) bijection via cardinal equality, but no explicit `T ≃ ℝ` map is built.

annotations.label: Cantor's 1878 bijection method

anchor.snippet: to construct a bijection between the closed interval

annotations.note: This specific historical interval-bijection construction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Dyadic numbers

anchor.snippet: These are called dyadic numbers and have the form

annotations.note: No general definition of dyadic numbers (of the form k/2^n) exists in Mathlib (only ad-hoc dyadic rationals inside Urysohn's lemma).

annotations.label: Bijection g(t) from T to (0,1)

anchor.snippet: Define the bijection g ( t ) from T to (0, 1)

annotations.note: `cantorFunction` provides the related summing-of-binary-digits map into ℝ, but the specific bijection onto `(0,1)` is not defined.

annotations.label: Final bijection T to R via tangent

anchor.snippet: which is a bijection from T to R

annotations.note: The composite bijection `T → (0,1) → ℝ` via the tangent function is not formalized as such.

annotations.label: Cantor's theorem

anchor.snippet: cannot be in bijection with S itself

annotations.note: `Cardinal.cantor : a < 2 ^ a` is Cantor's theorem that no set is in bijection with its power set.

annotations.label: Ordering of cardinalities

anchor.snippet: in terms of the existence of injections between

annotations.note: `le_def : #α ≤ #β ↔ Nonempty (α ↪ β)` defines cardinal order via existence of injections.

annotations.label: Schröder–Bernstein theorem

anchor.snippet: any two sets which are in the injective image of one another are in bijection as well

annotations.note: `schroeder_bernstein` gives a bijection from mutual injections `α → β` and `β → α`.

annotations.label: Set of all sets is inconsistent

anchor.snippet: the notion of the set of all sets is inconsistent

annotations.note: `not_surjective_Type` is the type-theoretic analog (why `Type : Type` is inconsistent) but the set-theoretic 'set of all sets' statement is not directly formalized.

annotations.label: Constructive uncountability

anchor.section: In the absence of excluded middle
anchor.snippet: these two collections are uncountable

annotations.note: Constructive (excluded-middle-free) uncountability results are not formalized; Mathlib works classically.

annotations.label: Schröder–Bernstein requires excluded middle

anchor.section: In the absence of excluded middle
anchor.snippet: the Schröder–Bernstein theorem requires the law of excluded middle

annotations.note: This metatheorem about the constructive non-provability of Schröder–Bernstein is not formalized.

annotations.label: Russell's paradox on unrestricted comprehension

anchor.snippet: set theory that includes an unrestricted comprehension scheme is contradictory

annotations.note: Russell's paradox / inconsistency of unrestricted comprehension is not formalized (Mathlib's `ZFSet` uses restricted separation).

annotations.label: Halting problem as diagonal argument

anchor.snippet: the conventional proof of the unsolvability of the halting problem is essentially a diagonal argument

annotations.note: `halting_problem` proves the predicate `(eval c n).Dom` is not computable, the unsolvability of the halting problem.

annotations.label: Diagonal set not a set in NF

anchor.snippet: is not a set

annotations.note: Quine's New Foundations and its stratification are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Modified diagonal set is a set in NF

anchor.snippet: is a set in NF

annotations.note: New Foundations stratified comprehension is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cardinality of P1(S) less than P(S)

anchor.snippet: use proof by contradiction to prove that

annotations.note: The underlying inequality `#S < #P(S)` is `Cardinal.cantor` (since P1(S) ≃ S), but the NF-typed formulation is not formalized.

annotations.label: P1(S) not in bijection with S by typing

anchor.snippet: It is not possible to put P 1 ( S ) in a one-to-one relation with S

annotations.note: This NF stratification/typing constraint argument is not formalized in Mathlib.
